answer:
Top 3 Test Data Factory Mistakes

Mistake 1: Hardcoding Test Data in Every Test

Copy-pasting the same mock objects across 50 test files. When the schema changes, you update 50 places.

// ❌ BAD: Duplicate data in every test
it('approves leave', () => {
  const leave = {
    id: 1, employeeId: 42, startDate: '2025-06-01',
    endDate: '2025-06-05', status: 'pending', type: 'casual',
    days: 5, reason: 'Vacation', createdAt: new Date()
  };
  // ... same object in 30 other test files
});

// ✅ GOOD: Centralized factory
import { createLeave } from '../factories/leaveFactory';

it('approves leave', () => {
  const leave = createLeave({ status: 'pending' }); // override only what matters
});

Mistake 2: Factories That Don't Generate Unique Data

Using static values causes unique constraint violations when creating multiple records.

// ❌ BAD: Static email causes unique constraint error
function createEmployee() {
  return { name: 'Test User', email: 'test@hr.com' }; // same email every time!
}

// ✅ GOOD: Unique data per call
let counter = 0;
function createEmployee(overrides = {}) {
  counter++;
  return {
    name: `Employee ${counter}`,
    email: `emp${counter}@hr.com`,
    role: 'employee',
    leaveBalance: 20,
    ...overrides
  };
}

Mistake 3: Over-Complicated Factories with Too Many Dependencies

Factories that create an entire object graph — employee + department + manager + leaves — when the test only needs an employee.

// ❌ BAD: Factory creates everything
async function createEmployee() {
  const dept = await prisma.department.create({ data: { ... } });
  const manager = await prisma.employee.create({ data: { ... } });
  const emp = await prisma.employee.create({ data: { departmentId: dept.id } });
  await prisma.leave.createMany({ data: [...5 leaves...] });
  return emp; // created 8 records just for one employee test!
}

// ✅ GOOD: Minimal factories, compose when needed
const createEmployee = (overrides = {}) => ({
  name: `Employee ${++seq}`,
  email: `emp${seq}@hr.com`,
  leaveBalance: 20,
  ...overrides
});

// Only create related data when the test actually needs it
const createEmployeeWithLeaves = async () => {
  const emp = await prisma.employee.create({ data: createEmployee() });
  await prisma.leave.create({ data: createLeave({ employeeId: emp.id }) });
  return emp;
};

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the test factory module exports createEmployee(), createLeave(), and createDepartment() with sensible defaults. Each test overrides only the fields relevant to its scenario. When the schema added a leaveType field, updating the factory in one place fixed all 80+ tests.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Factories that don't match the real schema. You add a required field in Prisma but forget to update the factory — tests pass with the old shape but production crashes.

Mitigation: Type your factories with the Prisma-generated types. TypeScript will error if the factory is missing a required field.

Definition

Test Data Factories are functions that generate test data with sensible defaults, letting tests override only the fields they care about. Instead of copy-pasting large mock objects in every test, factories centralize data creation — making tests cleaner, more maintainable, and schema-change resilient.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's ContactsHub, the Playwright E2E tests need test contacts with varying data — some with avatars, some without, some with specific tags. A factory function createContact({ hasAvatar: true, tags: ['vip'] }) generates complete contact objects, ensuring consistent test data across 60+ tests.

Visual Guide

Code Example

// factories/index.ts — Test data factories
let seq = 0;

export function createEmployee(overrides: Partial<Employee> = {}): Employee {
  seq++;
  return {
    id: seq,
    name: `Employee ${seq}`,
    email: `emp${seq}@hr.com`,
    role: 'employee',
    departmentId: 1,
    leaveBalance: 20,
    createdAt: new Date(),
    ...overrides // override any field for specific test scenarios
  };
}

export function createLeave(overrides: Partial<Leave> = {}): Leave {
  seq++;
  return {
    id: seq,
    employeeId: 1,
    startDate: new Date('2025-06-02'),
    endDate: new Date('2025-06-06'),
    type: 'casual',
    status: 'pending',
    days: 5,
    reason: 'Personal',
    createdAt: new Date(),
    ...overrides
  };
}

// Usage in tests — clean and focused
it('rejects leave when balance is insufficient', () => {
  const employee = createEmployee({ leaveBalance: 3 });
  const leave = createLeave({ days: 5 });
  
  const result = validateLeave(leave, employee);
  expect(result.valid).toBe(false);
});

it('approves leave when balance is sufficient', () => {
  const employee = createEmployee({ leaveBalance: 20 });
  const leave = createLeave({ days: 5 });
  
  const result = validateLeave(leave, employee);
  expect(result.valid).toBe(true);
});

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Creating factories for every tiny data structure. You don't need a factory for { id: 1, name: 'test' } — that's simple enough to inline.

Mitigation: Create factories for complex objects used in 5+ test files. Simple objects used in 1-2 tests can be inlined. The goal is reducing duplication, not adding abstraction for its own sake.

Definition

Contract Tests verify that two systems (e.g., frontend and backend API) agree on the shape of data they exchange — request/response formats, field names, types, and status codes. They catch breaking changes before deployment, without needing both systems running together.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the Angular frontend expects GET /api/leaves to return { data: [{ id: number, status: string, days: number }] }. If the backend changes days to duration, a contract test fails immediately — before the Angular app breaks in production.

Visual Guide

Code Example

// Contract definition (shared schema)
const LeaveResponseContract = {
  type: 'object',
  required: ['id', 'status', 'days', 'startDate', 'endDate', 'employeeId'],
  properties: {
    id: { type: 'number' },
    status: { type: 'string', enum: ['pending', 'approved', 'rejected'] },
    days: { type: 'number' },
    startDate: { type: 'string', format: 'date' },
    endDate: { type: 'string', format: 'date' },
    employeeId: { type: 'number' }
  }
};

// Backend test: Verify API matches contract
import Ajv from 'ajv';
const ajv = new Ajv();

it('GET /api/leaves returns data matching contract', async () => {
  const response = await app.inject({ method: 'GET', url: '/api/leaves' });
  const body = JSON.parse(response.body);

  const validate = ajv.compile(LeaveResponseContract);
  body.data.forEach(leave => {
    const valid = validate(leave);
    expect(valid).toBe(true);
    if (!valid) console.log(validate.errors);
  });
});

// Frontend test: Verify API client expects same shape
it('LeaveService maps API response correctly', () => {
  const mockResponse = { id: 1, status: 'pending', days: 5,
    startDate: '2025-06-01', endDate: '2025-06-05', employeeId: 42 };

  const validate = ajv.compile(LeaveResponseContract);
  expect(validate(mockResponse)).toBe(true);

  const mapped = leaveService.mapResponse(mockResponse);
  expect(mapped.daysRemaining).toBeDefined();
});

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Maintaining contract files manually — they drift from the actual API over time.

Mitigation: Generate contracts from the source of truth: use Fastify's JSON Schema validation (already in your route definitions) as the contract, or generate OpenAPI specs and test against them.

Definition

Contract Tests solve the API breaking change problem. In a team where frontend and backend developers work independently, one side can accidentally change the API shape — renaming a field, changing a type, removing a property — without the other side knowing. Contract tests catch these mismatches at test time, not in production.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's CRM Sales Dashboard, the Angular frontend consumed 8 API endpoints. When the backend developer renamed leadStatus to status for consistency, the frontend broke silently — the lead pipeline showed all leads as "unknown status." A contract test would have caught this rename in CI before merge.

Problems Solved

  • Breaking change detection: Catch field renames, type changes, and removed properties before deployment
  • Independent deployments: Frontend and backend can deploy separately with confidence
  • Documentation: The contract serves as a living API specification
  • Version safety: When versioning APIs (v1 → v2), contracts verify backward compatibility

Code Example

// Using Zod schemas as contracts (shared between frontend + backend)
import { z } from 'zod';

// Shared contract
export const LeaveSchema = z.object({
  id: z.number(),
  employeeId: z.number(),
  status: z.enum(['pending', 'approved', 'rejected', 'cancelled']),
  startDate: z.string().date(),
  endDate: z.string().date(),
  days: z.number().positive(),
  type: z.enum(['casual', 'sick', 'earned']),
  reason: z.string().optional()
});

export const LeaveListResponse = z.object({
  data: z.array(LeaveSchema),
  total: z.number(),
  page: z.number()
});

// Backend test: API output matches contract
it('GET /api/leaves matches LeaveListResponse contract', async () => {
  const response = await app.inject({ method: 'GET', url: '/api/leaves' });
  const result = LeaveListResponse.safeParse(JSON.parse(response.body));
  expect(result.success).toBe(true);
  if (!result.success) console.log(result.error.issues);
});

// Frontend test: Component handles contract-valid data
it('LeaveList renders contract-valid data', () => {
  const mockData = LeaveSchema.parse({
    id: 1, employeeId: 42, status: 'pending',
    startDate: '2025-06-01', endDate: '2025-06-05',
    days: 5, type: 'casual'
  });
  render(<LeaveList leaves={[mockData]} />);
  expect(screen.getByText('pending')).toBeInTheDocument();
});

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Contract tests that only check the happy path — valid responses. They don't verify error response shapes, which also need contracts.

Mitigation: Define contracts for error responses too: { error: string, code: string, details?: object }. Test that 400, 401, 404, and 500 responses all match the error contract.

Definition

Flaky Test Debugging is the practice of identifying and fixing tests that pass and fail intermittently without any code changes. A flaky test might pass 9 out of 10 times — eroding team confidence in the test suite. Common causes: timing issues, shared state, random data, environment dependencies.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's ContactsHub, a Playwright E2E test for CSV upload failed ~20% of the time in CI. The test clicked the upload button and immediately checked for a success message, but the file processing took 1-3 seconds depending on CI load. Adding await expect(page.locator('.toast-success')).toBeVisible({ timeout: 10000 }) made the test reliably wait for completion.

Visual Guide

Code Example

// Flaky test patterns and fixes

// ❌ FLAKY: Timing-dependent
test('shows notification after save', async ({ page }) => {
  await page.click('#save');
  const toast = page.locator('.toast');
  expect(await toast.textContent()).toBe('Saved!'); // might not be rendered yet!
});

// ✅ FIXED: Wait for element
test('shows notification after save', async ({ page }) => {
  await page.click('#save');
  await expect(page.locator('.toast')).toHaveText('Saved!'); // auto-waits
});

// ❌ FLAKY: Shared state between tests
let testUser;
beforeAll(() => { testUser = createUser(); }); // shared across all tests

test('deletes user', async () => {
  await deleteUser(testUser.id); // deletes the shared user
});

test('updates user', async () => {
  await updateUser(testUser.id, { name: 'New' }); // FAILS: user deleted!
});

// ✅ FIXED: Each test creates its own data
test('deletes user', async () => {
  const user = await createUser(); // fresh user for this test
  await deleteUser(user.id);
});

// ❌ FLAKY: Date-dependent
test('shows today\'s leaves', () => {
  const leaves = getLeaves(new Date()); // fails on weekends!
});

// ✅ FIXED: Use fixed dates
test('shows leaves for a weekday', () => {
  const monday = new Date('2025-06-02');
  const leaves = getLeaves(monday); // deterministic
});

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: "Fixing" flaky tests by adding retries (retries: 3). This masks the real problem — the test is still unreliable, it just passes by luck.

Mitigation: Use retries as a temporary measure in CI, but always investigate and fix the root cause. Track flaky tests with a report and dedicate time to fix them systematically.

Definition

Flaky Test Debugging solves the CI trust problem. When the test suite has flaky tests, developers stop trusting test failures — they re-run CI hoping it passes, merge with failing tests, or disable tests entirely. This defeats the purpose of automated testing.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's ContactsHub, the CI pipeline had 3 flaky Playwright tests out of 60+. Developers started adding "retry" comments on PRs, and once a real bug slipped through because the team assumed the failure was "just flaky." After fixing all 3 flaky tests (2 timing issues, 1 shared state), the team regained trust in CI — failures meant real bugs.

Problems Solved

  • CI reliability: Green means good, red means broken — no ambiguity
  • Developer productivity: No time wasted re-running CI hoping for green
  • Bug detection: Real failures are immediately investigated instead of dismissed
  • Merge confidence: Teams can enforce "all tests pass before merge" without friction

Code Example

// Debugging strategy for flaky tests

// Step 1: Identify flaky tests — run 10 times
// npx playwright test --repeat-each=10 e2e/contacts/upload.spec.ts

// Step 2: Use Playwright trace to see what happened
// playwright.config.ts
use: {
  trace: 'on', // capture trace for EVERY test run
  video: 'retain-on-failure'
}

// Step 3: Common fixes

// Fix timing: Use web-first assertions
await expect(page.locator('[data-testid="contact-count"]'))
  .toHaveText('500 contacts', { timeout: 15000 });

// Fix shared state: Use test isolation
test.describe.configure({ mode: 'serial' }); // if order matters
// OR better: each test creates its own data

// Fix network: Mock unstable external APIs
await page.route('**/api/external/**', route =>
  route.fulfill({ status: 200, body: JSON.stringify(mockData) })
);

// Fix animations: Disable CSS animations in test
await page.addStyleTag({ content: '*, *::after, *::before { animation: none !important; transition: none !important; }' });

// Step 4: Quarantine flaky tests until fixed
test.fixme('known flaky: CSV upload timing', async ({ page }) => {
  // Skipped but tracked — don't delete!
});

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Marking flaky tests as test.skip() and forgetting about them. Over time, you accumulate 20+ skipped tests that never get fixed.

Mitigation: Use test.fixme() (Playwright) or it.todo() (Jest) with a description of the flaky behavior. Track flaky tests in a dashboard or issue tracker. Set a team rule: fix or remove within 2 sprints.

Top 3 Coverage Quality Mistakes

Mistake 1: Chasing 100% Line Coverage Without Testing Behavior

Writing tests that execute every line but don't assert anything meaningful.

// ❌ BAD: 100% coverage, 0% confidence
it('runs calculateBalance', () => {
  calculateBalance(employee); // no assertions!
  // Every line is "covered" but nothing is verified
});

// ✅ GOOD: Meaningful assertions
it('deducts approved leave days from balance', () => {
  const result = calculateBalance({
    totalAllowance: 20,
    approvedLeaves: [{ days: 5 }, { days: 3 }]
  });
  expect(result.remaining).toBe(12); // 20 - 5 - 3
  expect(result.used).toBe(8);
});

Mistake 2: Not Testing Edge Cases and Error Paths

Only testing the happy path gives high coverage numbers but misses the code that handles errors, edge cases, and boundary conditions.

// ❌ BAD: Only happy path (misses 40% of branches)
it('creates a leave', async () => {
  const result = await createLeave(validData);
  expect(result.status).toBe('pending');
});
// Missing: invalid dates, insufficient balance, overlapping leaves,
// unauthorized user, database errors...

// ✅ GOOD: Test edge cases
it('rejects leave starting on Saturday', () => {
  const result = validateLeave({ startDate: new Date('2025-03-08') }); // Saturday
  expect(result.errors).toContain('Cannot start leave on weekend');
});

it('handles zero balance correctly', () => {
  const result = validateLeave({ balance: 0, days: 1 });
  expect(result.errors).toContain('Insufficient balance');
});

Mistake 3: Using Coverage as a Gate Without Branch Coverage

Enforcing 80% line coverage but not branch coverage. If-else blocks can have the "if" covered but not the "else."

// Code:
function getApprover(leave) {
  if (leave.days > 5) return 'manager';  // tested ✓
  else return 'teamLead';                // never tested ✗
}
// Line coverage: 100% (all lines executed)
// Branch coverage: 50% (only one branch tested)

// ✅ Fix: Test both branches
it('assigns manager for leaves > 5 days', () => {
  expect(getApprover({ days: 7 })).toBe('manager');
});
it('assigns team lead for leaves <= 5 days', () => {
  expect(getApprover({ days: 3 })).toBe('teamLead');
});

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the team initially had 85% line coverage but only 55% branch coverage. The leave validation function had 8 conditional paths but only 3 were tested. After adding tests for all branches (weekend check, overlap detection, balance check, max days check), branch coverage reached 90% and 2 bugs were found in the untested paths.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Coverage numbers becoming a vanity metric. Teams write meaningless tests to hit the 80% target without improving code quality.

Mitigation: Focus on branch coverage over line coverage. Mandate meaningful assertions. Use mutation testing (Stryker) to verify that tests actually catch bugs, not just execute code.

Definition

Coverage Quality measures not just how much code is tested (coverage percentage), but how well it's tested — whether tests actually catch bugs. High coverage with weak assertions gives false confidence. True coverage quality means every tested path has meaningful assertions that would fail if the code behavior changed.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's ContactsHub, the Jest coverage report showed 82% line coverage. But a mutation testing run (Stryker) revealed that 35% of the "covered" code could be changed without any test failing — meaning those tests ran the code but didn't verify its output. After improving assertions, the mutation score rose from 65% to 88%.

Visual Guide

Code Example

// jest.config.ts — Coverage thresholds
module.exports = {
  collectCoverage: true,
  coverageThresholds: {
    global: {
      lines: 80,
      branches: 75,    // ← More important than lines!
      functions: 80,
      statements: 80
    },
    // Stricter thresholds for critical modules
    './src/services/leaveService.ts': {
      branches: 90,
      lines: 95
    }
  },
  coverageReporters: ['text', 'lcov', 'html']
};

// Run with coverage
// npx jest --coverage

// Output:
// leaveService.ts    | 95% lines | 90% branches | 92% functions
// leaveValidator.ts  | 88% lines | 85% branches | 90% functions
// utils.ts           | 100% lines| 100% branches| 100% functions

// Mutation testing with Stryker (verifies test quality)
// npx stryker run
// Mutant survived: changed "balance < days" to "balance <= days"
// → This means no test catches the off-by-one error!

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Setting coverage gates too high (100%) causes developers to write meaningless tests just to hit the number, or exclude files from coverage to maintain the metric.

Mitigation: Set realistic thresholds (80% lines, 75% branches for the project, higher for critical paths). Focus code review on test quality (assertions) rather than coverage numbers. Use mutation testing quarterly to audit test effectiveness.
